---
title: "Quantile Regression in R"
subtitle: "Estimation, Visualisation and Interpretation"
author: "Tatjana Kecojević"
date: "24 June 2026"

bibliography: references.bib

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: serif
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    footer: "Beyond the Mean · Tatjana Kecojević"
    logo: "UoM_logos/UoM_logo_TAB_col_white_background.png"
    smaller: false
    scrollable: false
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    highlight-style: github
    fig-align: center

execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: false
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(quantreg)
library(AER)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 14))

set.seed(4821)

check_fn <- function(u, tau) u * (tau - as.numeric(u < 0))
taus <- c(0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90)
```

# Part 5 {background-color="#2C3E50"}

---

**Learning Objectives**:    

::: incremental
- Load and explore the CPS1988 dataset
- Fit an OLS baseline model for log wages
- Compare OLS and median regression estimates
- Fit quantile regression models using `rq()`
- Interpret confidence intervals and coefficient plots
- Describe how the return to education varies across the wage distribution
- Describe how the ethnicity wage gap varies across the wage distribution
- Interpret and communicate quantile regression results
:::

## The CPS1988 Dataset

Throughout this session we use the: <span style="color:#1f77b4;">CPS1988 Dataset</span>

The data contain information on:

- hourly wages
- education
- labour market experience
- ethnicity
- demographic characteristics

Our goal is to examine how these factors relate to wages across the distribution.

## Exploring the Wage Distribution

![](figures/wage_histograms.png)

## Why Log Wages?

- Weekly wages are strongly right-skewed.
- A small number of high earners create a long upper tail.
- Log wages are more symmetric.
- We therefore model log wages rather than wages in levels.

This is a reason why labour economists often model log wages rather than wages in levels.

---

## Wages by Ethnicity

![](figures/wage_ethnicity_density.png)

**Wage differences appear across the entire distribution.**

## Education and Experience

![](figures/wage_scatterplots.png)

**Education and experience are both associated with wages.**

## An OLS Baseline Model

Before fitting quantile regression models, we begin with a standard OLS specification.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(wage_i) =\;& \beta_0 +
\beta_1 education_i \\
&+ \beta_2 experience_i \\
&+ \beta_3 experience_i^2 \\
&+ \beta_4 ethnicity_i \\
&+ \varepsilon_i
\end{aligned}
$$

OLS estimates the average relationship between predictors and log wages.

---

## OLS Results

OLS estimates the average association between predictors and log wages.

Key findings:

- More education is associated with higher wages.
- Experience is positively related to wages.
- Wage growth slows at higher levels of experience.
- A wage gap remains after controlling for education and experience.

OLS provides a useful summary of average effects.

Do average effects tell the whole story?

---

## Moving Beyond the Mean

OLS estimates: $E(Y|X)$

Quantile regression allows us to estimate: $Q_\tau(Y|X)$

different points of the conditional wage distribution.

This lets us ask:

::: incremental
- Is the return to education larger among high earners?
- Does the ethnicity wage gap vary across the distribution?
- Are effects concentrated at the top or bottom of the wage distribution?
:::

---

## Median Regression

The median regression model estimates the conditional median:

$$
Q_{0.5}(Y|X)
$$

rather than the conditional mean.

It is the simplest quantile regression model and provides a useful comparison with OLS.

---

## Beyond the Median

Median regression focuses on the 50th percentile.

Quantile regression generalises this idea by allowing us to estimate:

$$
Q_{\tau}(Y|X)
$$

for different values of $\tau$.

Common choices are: $\tau = 0.10$, $\tau = 0.25$, $\tau = 0.50$, $\tau = 0.75$, $\tau = 0.90$

Together these provide a more complete picture of the conditional wage distribution.

---

## Quantile Regression Models

We estimate wage equations at several quantiles:

$$
\tau = 0.10,\;0.25,\;0.50,\;0.75,\;0.90
$$

Each model describes how predictors relate to a different part of the wage distribution.

Instead of one regression line, we obtain a series of distributional effects.

## Comparing OLS and Median Regression

Both models estimate relationships between predictors and wages.

However:

- OLS estimates the conditional mean.
- Median regression estimates the conditional median.
- Median regression is less sensitive to extreme observations.

In skewed wage distributions, these estimates need not be identical.

---

## Why Estimate Multiple Quantiles?

Median regression tells us about the centre of the distribution.

But social scientists are often interested in questions such as:

::: incremental
- Who benefits most from education?
- Are wage gaps larger among high earners?
- Do labour market advantages accumulate at the top?
:::

To answer these questions, we estimate several quantile regression models.

---

## Education Effects Across Quantiles

![](figures/education_return_ci.png)

**The estimated return to education varies across the wage distribution.**

---

## Interpreting the Education Effect

::: incremental
- Education is positively associated with wages across all quantiles.
- The estimated effect is larger at higher quantiles.
- Returns to education appear strongest among high-wage workers.
:::

**The benefits of education are not evenly distributed across workers.**

---

## Ethnicity Wage Gaps Across Quantiles

![](figures/ethnicity_wage_gap.png)

**The ethnicity wage gap varies across the wage distribution.**

---

## Interpreting the Ethnicity Effect

::: incremental
- Wage gaps are present across much of the distribution.
- The magnitude of the gap varies by quantile.
- Average effects conceal important distributional differences.
:::

**Quantile regression reveals where disparities are most pronounced.**

---

## Coefficient Process Plot

![](figures/coefficient_process_plot.png)

**Estimated effects change across quantiles.**

---

## Comparing Quantile and OLS Estimates

The coefficient process plot allows us to compare:

- OLS estimates (average effects)
- Quantile regression estimates (distributional effects)

When the quantile estimates depart from the OLS line, the effect varies across the distribution.

---

## Main Findings

::: incremental
- Wage distributions are highly skewed.
- OLS provides useful summaries of average effects.
- Education effects increase across the wage distribution.
- Ethnicity wage gaps vary across quantiles.
- Quantile regression reveals heterogeneity hidden by mean regression.
:::

---

## Key Takeaways

Quantile regression extends standard regression by modelling:

$$
Q_{\tau}(Y|X)
$$

rather than only:

$$
E(Y|X)
$$

This allows researchers to investigate how relationships differ across the outcome distribution.

---

## Questions? {background-color="#2C3E50"}


## 

::: {style="text-align: center; padding-top: 80px; font-size: 1.1em; font-style: italic; color: #555;"}

“All models are wrong, but some are useful.”

— George Box

:::
